title: Color Contrast — Minimum 4.5:1 for Normal Text
context: Apply to all text — labels, table cells, form fields, error messages, chart labels, navigation items. Test both light and dark modes independently.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 5 Visual Treatments That Improve Accessibility, W3C WCAG 2.1, ROSSUL — Enterprise UX Best Practices, Eleken — Bulk Action UX

title: Never Rely on Color Alone to Convey Information
context: Use when designing status indicators, data visualizations, charts, and any color-coded system.
sources: 100 Things #11 (New Riders, 2011), Nielsen Norman Group — 5 Visual Treatments That Improve Accessibility

title: Full Keyboard Accessibility for All Interactive Elements
context: Apply to all interactive elements. Keyboard navigation is critical for users with motor impairments and is also the preferred method for many enterprise power users.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Keyboard-Only Navigation for Improved Accessibility, Good UI — Keyboard Shortcuts, W3C WCAG 2.1

title: Visible Focus Indicators
context: Use for all interactive elements: links, buttons, inputs, custom components in all application states.
sources: W3C WCAG 2.1, Level Access — WCAG 2.1

title: Semantic HTML and ARIA Roles
context: Use for all UI component development — the foundation of accessible interface implementation.
sources: W3C WCAG 2.1

title: Keyboard Navigation with Tab Focus Management
context: Every enterprise application. Non-negotiable for WCAG AA compliance and a strong productivity feature for power users who prefer keyboard-first interaction.
sources: Eleken — Bulk Action UX, UX Patterns — Data Table

title: Keyboard-Navigable Modals and Focus Trapping
context: All modal dialogs, drawer panels, and dropdown menus across the application.
sources: Carbon Design System — Dialogs

title: Meaningful Alternative Text for Images and Data Visualizations
context: Apply to all images, icons, charts, and graphic elements. ALT text for data visualizations must describe the insight conveyed, not the chart type.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 5 Visual Treatments That Improve Accessibility

title: Minimum Target Size — 44×44px
context: Use for all interactive controls, especially those in dense data tables, toolbars, and compact table row actions.
sources: Level Access — WCAG 2.1 New Criteria

title: Respect prefers-reduced-motion
context: Use for all CSS and JavaScript animations, transitions, and scroll-triggered effects in the application.
sources: AlterSquare — Microinteractions

title: Keyboard-Accessible Drag and Drop
context: All drag-and-drop interactions. WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.1 requires all functionality to be operable via keyboard — drag-and-drop without keyboard equivalents is a compliance failure.
sources: Smart Interface Design Patterns — Drag and Drop UX, Eleken — Drag and Drop UI

title: Data Visualization Accessibility
context: All charts, status indicators, heatmaps, and any visualization using color to encode data.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Treemaps, Pencil & Paper — Dashboard UX

title: Cultural Context in Color and Symbol Interpretation
context: Use when designing for international or global enterprise markets, and when choosing status colors and iconography.
sources: 100 Things #12, #39 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Recognition Rather Than Recall
context: Apply in menu design, filtering interfaces, multi-step workflows, command interfaces, and any context where users must act on information from elsewhere in the UI.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, Good UI — Recognition Over Recall, 100 Things #22 (New Riders, 2011), Nielsen Norman Group — Heuristic 6

title: Hick's Law — Reduce Choices at Decision Points
context: Apply to navigation menus, filter panels, action menus, toolbar configurations, wizard steps, onboarding flows — any place where users must choose from competing options.
sources: Laws of UX — Hick's Law

title: Miller's Law — Chunk Information Into Groups of 5–7
context: Apply to navigation menus, filter chip groups, dashboard widget counts, table visible columns, form section groupings, and step indicators in wizards.
sources: Laws of UX — Miller's Law, Nielsen Norman Group — How Chunking Helps Content Processing

title: Jakob's Law — Leverage Familiar Patterns From Other Products
context: Apply when designing navigation, keyboard shortcuts, toolbar placement, form layouts, modal patterns, and data table conventions — especially when users migrate from known competing tools.
sources: Laws of UX — Jakob's Law, Nielsen Norman Group — Mental Model Inertia

title: Chunk Content Into Visually Distinct Groups
context: Use when designing data display, forms, reports, and help content — anywhere large amounts of information must be presented.
sources: 100 Things #27 (New Riders, 2011), Nielsen Norman Group — How Chunking Helps Content Processing, Laws of UX — Law of Proximity

title: Gestalt — Law of Common Region for Visual Grouping
context: Apply to dashboard card layouts, form section groupings, filter panels, sidebar modules, split-panel interfaces, property inspector panels — any UI where grouping must be communicated spatially.
sources: Laws of UX — Law of Common Region

title: Gestalt — Law of Similarity for Interactive Affordance
context: Apply to status badges, category labels, icon families, interactive vs. non-interactive elements, table column types, CTA vs. secondary button differentiation.
sources: Laws of UX — Law of Similarity

title: Offload User Tasks Through System Automation
context: Apply to form design, dashboard configuration, data import/export, and any repetitive operational task where the system can reduce user burden.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Minimize Cognitive Load to Maximize Usability

title: Build Mental Models Through Consistent Pattern Reuse
context: Apply to design system governance, new feature design, and any interaction that deviates from established patterns within the product.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Mental Model Inertia, Good UI — Consistency

title: Short-Term Memory Limits — Design for 4-Item Chunks
context: Use when designing multi-step workflows, any interface where users must remember information from a previous step, and wizard or multi-page form design.
sources: 100 Things #19–20 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Attention is Selective — Design for Salience
context: Use when designing alert and notification systems, and when evaluating why users miss important elements on a screen.
sources: 100 Things #40–41 (New Riders, 2011)

title: People Can't Multitask — Minimize Interruptions
context: Use when designing notification and interruption systems, and when deciding whether to integrate chat, alerts, or status feeds into focused work areas.
sources: 100 Things #46 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Choice Overload — Limit Visible Options
context: Use when designing configuration panels, filter interfaces, feature menus, and any context where users face a large number of options.
sources: 100 Things #92 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Cognitive Path of Least Resistance — Make the Intended Path Easiest
context: Use when evaluating whether users will follow intended workflows, and when users are creating workarounds to avoid standard processes.
sources: 100 Things #57–58 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Pattern Recognition and UI Consistency
context: Use when establishing UI component systems and when introducing new UI patterns in an established product.
sources: 100 Things #3 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Goal-Gradient Effect — Show Progress to Drive Completion
context: Use when designing multi-step onboarding, complex approval workflows, and any sequential process with completion as a goal.
sources: 100 Things #50 (New Riders, 2011), Laws of UX — Goal-Gradient Effect

title: Gestalt — Uniform Connectedness for Process and Relationship Visualization
context: Apply to process flows, pipeline visualizations, step-by-step wizards, dependency charts, relationship diagrams, and data lineage views in enterprise applications.
sources: Laws of UX — Law of Uniform Connectedness

title: Law of Prägnanz — Use Simplest Effective Visual Form
context: Apply to icon design, data visualization, dashboard layouts, form design, navigation structures — any place where visual clarity reduces interpretation time.
sources: Laws of UX — Law of Prägnanz

title: Visual Appearance Determines Initial Trust
context: Use when prioritizing visual polish investments and when a product will be evaluated by new users or in sales/procurement contexts.
sources: 100 Things #79 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Social Proof and Activity Signals for Feature Adoption
context: Use when designing onboarding for new users, configuration recommendations, and feature adoption flows where users are uncertain about what to choose.
sources: 100 Things #98 (New Riders, 2011), Good UI — Social Proof

title: Mood Influences Decision Quality — Design the Path to Decisions
context: Use when designing user flows leading to high-stakes decisions, and when evaluating performance and reliability requirements for critical-path features.
sources: 100 Things #95 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Variable Rewards Drive Authentic Engagement
context: Use when designing intelligence/recommendation features, proactive insights, and exploratory analytics features.
sources: 100 Things #51–52 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Sustained Attention Design — 10-Minute Pause Points
context: Use when designing long-form data entry workflows and complex multi-stage review processes.
sources: 100 Things #44 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Modals — Reserve for Critical Focus-Required Interactions
context: Apply when deciding whether to use a modal. The cost is always an interruption to the user's current task — justify it with the severity of the consequence.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Modal & Nonmodal Dialogs, Component Gallery — Modal / Dialog, About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 21 (Wiley, 2014), Eleken — Modal UX

title: Nonmodal Side Panels as Default for Non-Critical Interactions
context: Apply to record detail views, annotation panels, help documentation, search result previews, settings that don't require interruption — wherever users need context alongside the primary view.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Modal & Nonmodal Dialogs, Component Gallery — Drawer / Side Panel

title: Tabs — Use Only for Clear Independent Content Groupings
context: Apply to detail views with distinctly typed sections (Overview / Settings / History / Permissions), module organization in complex forms. Avoid when users need to compare content across tabs simultaneously.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Tabs, Used Right, Component Gallery — Tabs

title: Tooltips — Supplementary Clarification, Not Critical Information
context: Apply to all unlabeled icon buttons, abbreviated labels, technical terms, and form fields with non-obvious requirements. Never put task-critical information in a tooltip.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Tooltip Guidelines, Component Gallery — Tooltip

title: Distinct Visual States for Clickable, Selected, and Plain Elements
context: Apply to navigation systems, data table rows, button sets, filter chips, and any interactive component in a design system.
sources: Good UI — Distinct Clickable/Selected Styles

title: Toast Notifications — Transient Confirmation for Completed Actions
context: Apply to post-action confirmation (record saved, email sent, export complete), undo prompts after soft deletions, and brief system status updates that don't require user action.
sources: Component Gallery — Toast / Snackbar

title: Progress Bar — Determinate and Indeterminate Patterns
context: Apply to file uploads, bulk data imports/exports, report generation, batch processing, and multi-step wizard completion.
sources: Component Gallery — Progress Bar

title: Badge / Tag / Chip — Semantic Color and Label Consistency
context: Apply to status indicators on records (Active, Pending, Closed), category labels, count indicators, user role labels, and filter chips.
sources: Component Gallery — Badge / Tag / Chip

title: Combobox / Autocomplete for Long-List Dropdowns
context: Apply when a dropdown list has 10+ items — filtering dramatically reduces scan time compared to standard select dropdowns.
sources: Component Gallery — Combobox / Autocomplete]

title: Search Input Component Design Standards
context: Apply to global application search, in-table/list filtering, record lookup fields, and within-page text search.
sources: Component Gallery — Search Input

title: Descriptive, Concise Tab Labels
context: Apply to all tab labels in enterprise applications. Labels are navigation — they must predict content before the user clicks.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Tabs, Used Right

title: Disable, Don't Hide, Irrelevant Menu Items
context: Apply to role-based or context-sensitive contextual menus and toolbars in enterprise applications where item availability changes based on state or permissions.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Contextual Menus: Delivering Relevant Tools for Tasks

title: Popovers for Interactive Supplemental Content
context: Apply when supplemental content needs to be interactive (clickable, form-like) — use popovers for click-triggered interactive content, tooltips for hover-triggered non-interactive content.
sources: Component Gallery — Popover

title: Accordion — Collapsible Sections for Occasionally Needed Content
context: Apply to settings panels with many sections, FAQ sections, filtering panels with collapsible filter groups, and long detail pages where not all sections are always relevant.
sources: Component Gallery — Accordion

title: Tree View for Hierarchical Data Navigation
context: Apply to file system navigation, nested project/folder structures, organizational hierarchies, taxonomy editors, and settings with nested categories.
sources: Component Gallery — Tree View, Koruux — Enterprise Data Visualization

title: Toggle / Switch — Immediate-Effect Binary Settings
context: Apply to feature enable/disable settings, notification preferences, integration toggles, column visibility, and view mode switches — settings that take immediate effect.
sources: Component Gallery — Toggle / Switch

title: Design Dashboards Around User Goals — Not Available Data
context: Apply at the start of any dashboard design project — executive, operational, analytical, monitoring. The most common failure is starting with available data and cramming it in.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Visualizations for Dashboards

title: Preattentive Attributes for Fast Visual Processing in Dashboards
context: Apply to any data visualization — bar charts, line charts, KPI cards, status indicators — where users need to identify patterns, outliers, or status at a glance.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Visualizations for Dashboards

title: Dashboard Hover Tooltips for Contextual Detail on Demand
context: Apply to all charts and data visualizations where exact values matter alongside readable summary views.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Visualizations for Dashboards, Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications

title: Dashboard Customization and Personalization
context: Apply to enterprise dashboards used by users with varying roles, priorities, or workflows. A one-size-fits-none compromise drives low adoption.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Visualizations for Dashboards, UX Magazine — Enterprise UX, UX Planet — Data Table Best Practices, Excited Agency — Dashboard UX, Pencil & Paper — Dashboard UX

title: Card Component Anatomy for Dashboard KPIs
context: Apply to dashboard KPI widgets, record summary views, entity listing in grid view, and feature promotion panels.
sources: Component Gallery — Card, Laws of UX — Law of Common Region

title: Dashboard Information Hierarchy
context: Use when designing any dashboard with 3+ data widgets or KPI cards.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Enterprise Data Tables, About Face Ch. 13 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Contextual Filtering and Date Ranges on Dashboards
context: Use for any operational or analytical dashboard where data varies by time period, region, team, or other dimensions.
sources: Design Systems — Space, Grids, and Layouts

title: F-Pattern and Z-Pattern Dashboard Visual Hierarchy
context: Every dashboard. The reading hierarchy must be defined before any widget is placed. Poor placement of critical information is the most common dashboard design failure.
sources: Excited Agency — Dashboard UX Design, DataCamp — Effective Dashboard Design, Pencil & Paper — Dashboard UX

title: Metrics Bar — KPI Highlight Row
context: All operational and monitoring dashboards where a handful of KPIs dominate decision-making — sales dashboards, financial dashboards, operations centers.
sources: Salt Design System — Analytical Dashboard, DataCamp — Dashboard Design

title: Dashboard Type and Audience Matching
context: At the start of every dashboard design project. Mismatching dashboard type to use case is a primary cause of poor adoption in enterprise analytics.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Dashboard UX

title: Delta Indicators and Contextual Comparisons
context: Every quantitative KPI card on any dashboard. A standalone number without a reference point communicates nothing about whether performance is good or bad.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Dashboard UX, Smashing Magazine — Real-Time Dashboard UX

title: Data Freshness Indicators
context: Any dashboard or data view that refreshes automatically or may have a delay between the data source and the display. Critical in monitoring, financial, and operational contexts.
sources: Smashing Magazine — UX Strategies for Real-Time Dashboards

title: Data-Ink Ratio and Anti-Clutter
context: Every dashboard review cycle. Apply during QA before launch and at each design iteration. Decoration is the most common dashboard quality degrader over time.
sources: DataCamp — Effective Dashboard Design

title: Sparklines for Inline Trend Visualization
context: Dashboard KPI cards, data table columns with historical series, monitoring panels where trend direction is as important as the current value.
sources: Smashing Magazine — Real-Time Dashboard UX

title: Role-Based Dashboard Personalization
context: Any enterprise application serving 2+ distinct user roles with materially different primary metrics and tasks.
sources: Koruux — Enterprise Data Visualization, Smashing Magazine — Real-Time Dashboard UX

title: Alert and Threshold-Based Notifications
context: Any operational dashboard or monitoring tool where users need to be informed of anomalies — IT operations, financial monitoring, supply chain management, SLA management tools.
sources: Smashing Magazine — Real-Time Dashboard UX, Raw.Studio — UX Rules for Real-Time Dashboards

title: Comparison Mode for Time-Series Data
context: Any time-series chart on a monitoring or reporting dashboard where trend comparison is a common user task.
sources: DataCamp — Effective Dashboard Design

title: Subtle Micro-Animations for Live Data Updates
context: Real-time monitoring dashboards, live analytics, financial trading interfaces, operational control centers where data changes frequently and users need to notice changes without being distracted.
sources: Smashing Magazine — UX Strategies for Real-Time Dashboards

title: Pause/Freeze Option for Live Data Streams
context: High-frequency updating dashboards — stock tickers, real-time operational metrics, live event monitoring — where users need to read and analyze data without it changing underneath them.
sources: Smashing Magazine — UX Strategies for Real-Time Dashboards

title: Data Tables — Support All Four Core User Tasks by Design
context: Apply to any enterprise data table — records lists, transaction logs, user management, inventory, analytics exports. All four tasks will be performed regardless of whether the design supports them.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables: Four Major User Tasks, Component Gallery — Data Table

title: Frozen Headers and Visual Anchors in Large Tables
context: Apply to any table with more rows than fit on screen, or with more than ~8 columns. Users lose orientation during scroll without visual anchors.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables: Four Major User Tasks, UX Planet — Data Table Best Practices, Pencil & Paper — Data Table UX Patterns

title: Column Management for Comparison Work
context: Use for data tables with 8+ possible columns or where different user roles have different data priorities.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Enterprise Data Tables, Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables: Four Major User Tasks, Component Gallery — Data Table, UX Patterns — Data Table

title: Row-Level and Batch Actions Design in Tables
context: Apply to all enterprise data tables where users manage records — delete, archive, export, assign, approve, reject. Both single-record and batch workflows are essential.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables: Four Major User Tasks, Eleken — Bulk Action UX

title: Pagination vs. Infinite Scroll — Choose by Task Type
context: Use when deciding how users will navigate long lists — different content types and user tasks require different navigation patterns.
sources: UX Planet — Data Table Best Practices, Component Gallery — Pagination

title: Three-Level Density System for Tables and Lists
context: Use for any enterprise data table used in multiple contexts or by users with varying display needs and accessibility requirements.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Enterprise Data Tables

title: Correct Data Alignment in Tables
context: Use in all data tables containing numeric, financial, or quantitative data.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Enterprise Data Tables

title: Contextual Hover Actions in Data Tables
context: Use in all data tables with per-row actions (CRUD tables, management interfaces, list views).
sources: Pencil & Paper — Enterprise Data Tables

title: Sortable Columns with Clear State Indicators
context: Any table with data that users need to prioritize or compare — dates, values, statuses, names. Absent sorting, users must scan the entire table to find relevant records.
sources: UX Patterns for Developers — Data Table, Pencil & Paper — Data Table UX Patterns

title: Virtual Scrolling for Large Datasets
context: Tables with server-side data that may grow to thousands of rows. Critical in audit logs, transaction history, event streams, and large inventory tables.
sources: UX Patterns — Data Table

title: Inline Editing with Friction Calibration
context: All tables where data is editable. The choice of inline vs. modal editing should be driven by the risk and frequency of the edit operation.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Data Table UX Patterns, Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables

title: Expandable Row Details and Sidebar Panels
context: When records have significant associated detail — attachments, related records, history, notes. Common in CRM, support ticket systems, and order management.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Data Table UX Patterns, Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables

title: Tables Over Cards for Dense Multi-Variate Data
context: Apply when users need to compare attributes across many records simultaneously, detect outliers, or perform bulk actions. Cards are better for visual browsing with fewer comparison needs.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables: Four Major User Tasks

title: Smart Defaults for Sort Order
context: Use when designing any sortable data table — the default sort order should reflect the primary user task, not technical defaults.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Enterprise Data Tables

title: Column Order Reflecting Data Importance
context: All data tables. Collaborate with primary users during design to validate column priority before launch — column order has a larger impact on task performance than most other table design choices.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables

title: Row Density Controls
context: Any enterprise table used by power users or in data-heavy workflows. Standard in ERP, analytics, and financial applications where users range from data entry operators to occasional reviewers.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Data Table UX Patterns

title: Pagination vs. Virtual Scrolling Decision Framework
context: Any enterprise data table. The choice between pagination and scrolling has downstream effects on performance, shareability, and user orientation within the dataset.
sources: UX Patterns — Data Table

title: Expandable Row Detail (Inline Master-Detail)
context: When detail content is compact (3–10 fields or a small nested table) and users need to compare detail across multiple records simultaneously — order lines, BOM views, financial statement detail.
sources: Infragistics — Master-Detail Layout

title: Dense Numerical Display — Monospace and Column Alignment
context: All tables and data views with numerical data — financial reports, inventory counts, performance metrics, analytics tables.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Data Table UX Patterns

title: Treemap for Hierarchical Quantitative Data
context: When users need to compare relative sizes across a hierarchy — portfolio analysis, budget roll-ups, storage usage, sales composition. Use sparingly; only when hierarchy and proportion are both critical.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Treemaps

title: Conduct Deep User Research with End-Users, Not Just Executives
context: Apply to any enterprise product research activity — usability testing, requirements gathering, feedback collection. Executives are buyers; employees are users.
sources: UX Magazine — Enterprise UX: The Value of Usability

title: The Three Lenses of Human-Centered Design
context: Use at every major product or feature decision gate, especially when evaluating competing solution directions or validating whether to build something.
sources: IDEO Design Thinking

title: Goal-Directed Design — Design for Goals, Not Tasks
context: Use at the foundation of every product design initiative, and when evaluating whether a feature serves users or merely exposes internal system implementation.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 1 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Personas — Archetypal User Models Built from Research
context: Use before any design work begins, when teams disagree about user priorities, and as the primary design decision filter throughout a project lifecycle.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 3–4 (Wiley, 2014), IDEO Design Thinking

title: Design for the Perpetual Intermediate
context: Use when deciding how much assistance to provide by default, when balancing feature discoverability against information density, and when calibrating onboarding depth.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 3 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Double Diamond Design Process
context: Use for new feature development, product redesigns, and any time the team is tempted to jump directly to solutions without validating the problem.
sources: Amoeboids — Double Diamond Design Process

title: Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics — Evaluation Framework
context: Use before user testing to clear obvious issues, before major launches, after significant redesigns, and for competitive analysis.
sources: CleverX — Nielsen's 10 Heuristics

title: Usability Testing Methodology
context: Use before launching major new features, when significant redesign decisions are being made, and when the team disagrees about a design decision.
sources: CleverX — Nielsen's Heuristics and Usability Testing

title: Contextual Inquiry — User Research in Real Environments
context: Use at the start of any new feature or product initiative and when designing for specialized roles where work context strongly influences tool design.
sources: IDEO HCD Toolkit

title: Customer Journey Mapping for End-to-End Workflow Design
context: Use when designing multi-step workflows, when users report frustration but can't pinpoint where, and when planning a product-wide redesign.
sources: IDEO Design Thinking

title: Prototype Fidelity Matching the Question
context: Use at every prototyping decision point and when scoping prototype investment during sprint planning.
sources: IDEO Prototyping Principles

title: Show Don't Tell — Living Documentation
context: Use at every design-to-engineering handoff, when presenting design decisions to stakeholders, and when documenting design rationale.
sources: IDEO / Cantina

title: Radical Collaboration — Multidisciplinary Product Teams
context: Use when forming teams for complex product initiatives, and when design and engineering are working in silos.
sources: IDEO Design Thinking

title: Define Information Architecture Before Designing Navigation
context: Apply at the start of any product design or major redesign project — before any navigation UI design begins.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — IA vs. Navigation, Boxes and Arrows — Information Architecture: Blueprints for the Web

title: Card Sorting for User-Centric Information Architecture
context: Use when designing navigation, menus, or IA for a new product, when consolidating legacy systems, and when users report difficulty finding things.
sources: IDEO Method Cards, Nielsen Norman Group — IA vs. Navigation, Boxes and Arrows — Information Architecture: Blueprints for the Web

title: Quantify UX ROI — Measure Productivity Impact
subcategory: UX business case and ROI measurement
context: Apply when making the business case for UX investment, prioritizing redesign efforts, or selecting features with the highest UX impact.
sources: UX Magazine — Enterprise UX: The Value of Usability

title: Distinguish IA from Interaction Design — Design Both Explicitly
context: Apply at the start of any complex enterprise product design engagement, before wireframing begins.
sources: Boxes and Arrows — Introducing Interaction Design, Boxes and Arrows — Foundations of Interaction Design

title: Low-Fidelity Wireframes Early to Drive Critical Decisions
context: Apply at the start of any design project, before high-fidelity work begins. Rough wireframes surface critical design questions while changes are cheap.
sources: Boxes and Arrows — The Shallow Dive

title: Integrate UX Design Sprint Ahead of Development
context: Apply in any agile product development team where UX/design and engineering work in the same sprint cadence.
sources: Boxes and Arrows — Bringing UCD to the Agile Environment

title: Three Types of User Goals (Experience / End / Life)
context: Use during persona development and when users report that a product is functionally adequate but emotionally dissatisfying.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 4 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Breadboarding for Interaction Flow Design
context: Use during early shaping phase when defining navigation structure and key affordances for a new feature, before any visual design.
sources: Shape Up by Ryan Singer, Ch. 4 (Basecamp, 2019)

title: Affinity Diagrams for Research Synthesis
context: Use after completing a research sprint to synthesize findings from multiple sources before defining the problem statement.
sources: IDEO Method Cards

title: "How Might We" Problem Framing
context: Use after research synthesis, when a feature request arrives without clear user need context, and before any ideation session.
sources: IDEO Design Thinking / Stanford d.school

title: Diverge Before Converging — Separation of Ideation and Evaluation
context: Use at the start of every design challenge, when teams are stuck in incremental thinking, and before wireframing begins.
sources: IDEO Design Thinking

title: Design Critique Best Practices
context: Use before major design handoffs, at key decision points in the design process, and during design system evolution.
sources: Atlassian — Design Review Process

title: Empathy Mapping for Research Synthesis
context: Use after research interviews, as a team alignment tool before ideation, and when building or validating personas.
sources: IDEO Design Thinking

title: Aesthetic-Usability Effect — Visual Polish Affects Perceived Usability
context: Important when modernizing legacy internal tools or when your users have previously dismissed the product as "ugly and hard to use." Also critical at high-stakes moments: onboarding, dashboards, externally-shared reports.
sources: Laws of UX — Aesthetic-Usability Effect

title: Cores and Paths — Design From the Core Content Outward
context: Apply in any application where users have a clear primary goal — product lookup, transaction processing, record management, report viewing — to ensure navigation leads efficiently to that core.
sources: Boxes and Arrows — Designing Screens Using Cores and Paths

title: Process-Oriented Journey Maps for Enterprise Systems
context: Apply when designing or evaluating complex enterprise workflows spanning multiple roles, departments, or systems. Not applicable to single-screen or single-user interactions.
sources: UX Magazine — User Journey Mapping for Complex Enterprise Systems

title: Separate Page Content Priority From Visual Layout Decisions
context: Apply when documenting IA for complex sites and applications, especially when stakeholders focus on layout details rather than content structure during reviews.
sources: Boxes and Arrows — Where the Wireframes Are

title: Group Decision-Making Failure Modes — Structured Decision Processes
context: Use when running design review meetings, product prioritization sessions, and any group design decision with more than 3 participants.
sources: 100 Things #96–97 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Consistency and Standards
context: Apply in every design decision — users spend most of their time in other products, so their prior experiences set expectations for yours. Critical when building multi-module enterprise products.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, Good UI — Consistency, Good UI — Conventions, Laws of UX — Jakob's Law

title: 8pt Grid Spatial System
context: Use when establishing a design system from scratch, or when retrofitting spacing consistency onto an existing product with inconsistent spacing.
sources: Design Systems — Space, Grids, and Layouts

title: Semantic Color Tokens for Dark Mode Theme Switching
context: Use when implementing dark mode for the first time, or when the current theming implementation requires component-level changes to switch themes.
sources: YNAB — Semantic Color System

title: Typographic Scale Principles
context: Use when establishing a design system from scratch or when standardizing typography in an existing product with inconsistent type usage.
sources: Design Systems — Space, Grids, and Layouts

title: Status Color Consistency
context: Use in all status indicators, alerts, banners, form validation, badges, and data visualizations.
sources: Carbon Design System — Notification Pattern

title: Status Badge and State Indicator System
context: All enterprise applications with entities that have workflow states, health indicators, or priority levels. Must be defined in the design system, not ad hoc per component.
sources: Smashing Magazine — Real-Time Dashboard UX

title: Three-Layer Design Token Architecture
context: Use when establishing a design system serving multiple products, platforms, or teams.
sources: Martin Fowler — Design Token Architecture

title: Component Variants via Properties (Not Separate Components)
context: Use when building design system components with multiple visual variations in size, style, and state.
sources: Penpot — Component Variants

title: Composition Over Configuration in Design Systems
context: Use when building complex design system components that need to be highly flexible and customizable without forking.
sources: ITNEXT — SOLID Principles for Design Systems

title: Flexibility and Efficiency — Power User Acceleration
context: Apply in enterprise tools used daily by power users — data management, analytics, CRMs, ERP systems. The 20% of frequent users who perform 80% of operations need accelerated pathways.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, Good UI — Keyboard Shortcuts, Laws of UX — Pareto Principle

title: High Information Density for Expert Enterprise Users
context: Use when designing for professional desktop users who spend the majority of their workday in the application, particularly analysts, operators, and clinicians.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 9 (Wiley, 2014), YouTube: 8 Principles for Complex Desktop Apps

title: Sovereign Posture — Design for Full-Attention Desktop Apps
context: Use when designing desktop B2B/enterprise tools where users spend extended periods — 4+ hours per day — exclusively within the application.
sources: About Face Ch. 9 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Smart Product Memory — Persist User Configurations
context: Use when designing application state persistence, search and filter interfaces, and any configuration that users set routinely.
sources: About Face Ch. 12 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Desktop-First Design for Enterprise Applications
context: Use for enterprise applications where primary usage is on desktop monitors and where the full feature set depends on horizontal screen space.
sources: Interaction Design Foundation — Responsive Design

title: Master-Detail Side-by-Side Layout
context: Email clients, CRM record lists, document libraries, ticket queues, order management — any context where users switch frequently between items in a list.
sources: Microsoft Windows Developer Blog — Master the Master-Detail Pattern, Appli — The Master-Detail Interface Pattern

title: Comprehensive Keyboard Shortcut System
context: All enterprise desktop applications, especially those used daily for extended sessions — ERP, CRM, project management, analytics tools. Power users experience 20–40% productivity gains from shortcuts.
sources: LinkedIn — Shortcut Productivity Hacks, Sparx Systems — Enterprise Architect Keyboard Shortcuts

title: Resizable Split-Pane Layout
context: Master-detail, editor+preview, tree+detail, list+form — any layout where two related content areas benefit from simultaneous visibility.
sources: Microsoft Docs — Split View, OnePageCRM — Split Pane Design Challenges

title: Contextual Toolbar Actions
context: Any application with a main toolbar or action bar where applicable actions change based on selection state.
sources: Sparx Systems — Enterprise Architect Ribbons

title: Context Menu Placement and Consistency
context: Any UI element with secondary actions that would clutter the primary interface — table rows, list items, file nodes, canvas objects.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Contextual Menus Guidelines, LogRocket — Creating Context Menus

title: Context-Sensitive Menu Content
context: All context menus. The value of a context menu is its contextual relevance — a generic menu defeats its purpose.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Contextual Menus Guidelines

title: Surface Efficient Methods In Context — Combat the Productivity Plateau
context: Apply in tools with advanced capabilities that most users never discover — analytics platforms, spreadsheet-like editors, CRMs, data modeling tools.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications

title: Help Users Track Actions and Thought Processes — Audit Logs and Notes
subcategory: Working memory offloading for complex data work
context: Apply to data analysis, research, configuration, and investigation workflows where context must be preserved across long or interrupted sessions.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications

title: Multiple Pathways to the Same Goal
context: Use when designing navigation, action models, and keyboard shortcut systems for applications used heavily by power users.
sources: YouTube: 8 Principles for Complex Desktop Apps, About Face Ch. 18 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Dark Mode — Dark Gray, Not Pure Black
context: Use in all dark mode implementations.
sources: Codener — Dark Mode Best Practices 2024

title: Elevation Through Lightness in Dark Mode
context: Use for all dark mode surface components: base canvas, cards, dropdowns, modals, tooltips.
sources: Codener — Dark Mode Best Practices 2024

title: Toolbars — Frequency, Context, Grouping
context: Use when designing application toolbars for desktop enterprise tools and when reviewing toolbar complexity complaints.
sources: About Face Ch. 18 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Menu Design — Predictable Hierarchy, Keyboard Shortcuts
context: Use when designing menu structures for desktop applications and when reviewing menu complexity for findability.
sources: About Face Ch. 18 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Contextual Help Over Static Documentation
context: Use when designing for specialist users who need to learn domain-specific interactions, and when evaluating the onboarding experience for complex features.
sources: YouTube: 8 Principles for Complex Desktop Apps

title: Overlay vs. Inline Split Pane Modes
context: Navigation sidebars, property panels, secondary toolboxes. Choose the mode based on whether the pane's content is primary (inline) or supplementary (overlay).
sources: Microsoft Docs — Split View Controls

title: Visual Density Control (Application-Level)
context: All enterprise data-heavy applications where power users (data entry, operations) and occasional users (executives, reviewers) coexist in the same team.
sources: Envylabs — Interface Information Density

title: Responsive Layout for Enterprise Desktop
context: All enterprise desktop application layouts. Monitor sizes range from 1280×800 laptops to 4K external monitors — a 4× width range that demands responsive treatment.
sources: UXmatters — Data-Heavy User Experiences

title: Panel Docking and Undocking
context: Complex professional-grade applications — CAD tools, BI platforms, IDEs, data modeling tools, financial terminals — where users spend significant time and have strong workflow preferences.
sources: DevExpress — WPF Ribbon & Toolbars

title: Quick Access Toolbar (QAT)
context: Applications with ribbon/tab-based navigation or deep hierarchical menus where frequently used actions are buried several levels deep.
sources: DevExpress — WPF Ribbon & Toolbars

title: Ribbon/Tab-Based Toolbar for Feature-Rich Applications
context: Desktop applications with 50+ distinct commands — document editors, CAD tools, data modeling platforms. Not recommended for simpler SaaS-style web applications.
sources: DevExpress — WPF Ribbon & Toolbars

title: High-Density Interface Design Principles
context: Any application requiring power users to process large amounts of data quickly — financial trading platforms, air traffic control, medical imaging systems, analytics workbenches.
sources: LinkedIn — Rethinking Density in UX

title: Enterprise Onboarding — Self-Service Success Without Training
context: Apply to the first-time use experience for any enterprise application. "A little training is required" is not acceptable at consumer-grade UX standards.
sources: UX Magazine — The Future of Enterprise Design Is Consumer-Grade UX, UX Magazine — Enterprise UX: The Value of Usability

title: End-User Customization Without IT Involvement
context: Apply to any enterprise feature with significant variation in how different users or teams want to use it — dashboards, report views, notification preferences, workflow templates.
sources: UX Magazine — The Future of Enterprise Design Is Consumer-Grade UX

title: Target Consumer-Grade Usability Standards in Enterprise Products
context: Apply as a quality standard for all enterprise product design. Teams and employees increasingly choose their own tools — products win or lose based on experience quality, not sales cycles.
sources: UX Magazine — The Future of Enterprise Design Is Consumer-Grade UX

title: Reduce Clutter Without Reducing Capability
context: Apply to configuration panels, advanced settings, and data-dense views with many infrequently used controls in enterprise applications.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications

title: Design for Interruption — Autosave and Session Resumability
context: Apply to all complex workflows, forms, and wizards in enterprise applications. Interruptions are not exceptional — they are the norm in enterprise work environments.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications, Laws of UX — Zeigarnik Effect

title: Bulk Actions for Power Users
context: Use in any data table or list where users regularly need to manage multiple items — CRMs, order management, content management systems.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Enterprise Data Tables

title: Role-Based Interface Adaptation (RBAC in UI)
context: Use in any multi-role enterprise application where different user types have meaningfully different workflows and capabilities.
sources: Nakisa — RBAC in Enterprise Software

title: Permission-Based UI Rendering (Least Privilege)
context: Any enterprise application with multiple user roles. Especially important in multi-tenant systems and applications handling sensitive financial, HR, or compliance data.
sources: Glance Group — How Do You Implement RBAC in Enterprise Apps, Desilo Studio — IA for Complex SaaS

title: Permission-Aware Navigation Design
context: Any multi-role application with role-specific sections. The goal is that users should never navigate to a page only to receive an "Access Denied" error.
sources: Desilo Studio — IA for Complex SaaS

title: Comprehensive Audit Trail Architecture
context: All enterprise applications handling sensitive data, financial transactions, user management, or regulated content. Mandatory for healthcare, finance, legal, and government sectors.
sources: Shyft — Mastering Audit Trail Architecture, Workato — Activity Audit Log

title: Always-Available CSV/Excel Export
context: All data tables, reports, and dashboards. Data ownership and portability are core enterprise expectations — restricting exports degrades trust.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Dashboard UX

title: Admin Panel Multi-Column Interface
context: All back-office and administrative interfaces. The multi-column structure is the established baseline for admin panel design in enterprise software.
sources: Aspirity — Admin Panel Design Best Practices

title: Inline Tooltips and Contextual Help for Admin UIs
context: All admin settings with non-obvious behavior — permission settings, integration configurations, billing controls, and data management operations.
sources: Aspirity — Admin Panel Design Best Practices

title: Coordinate Transitions Across Multiple Tools and Workspaces
context: Apply to enterprise tools where users must combine data from multiple systems — analytics + spreadsheets + reporting, CRM + email + calendar.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications

title: Ease Transition Between Primary and Secondary Information
context: Apply to data tables with linked detail records, dashboards with drill-down data, and complex forms with field-level help.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications

title: Graceful Permission Denial
context: Use when a user attempts an action or reaches a page they don't have permission to access.
sources: Cerbos — Enterprise Authorization Models

title: Role-Resource-Action Permission Matrix
context: During initial RBAC system design for any enterprise application. Serves as the single source of truth for all UI conditional rendering decisions throughout development.
sources: NocoBase — How to Design an RBAC System

title: Tenant Isolation and Context Clarity
context: Any multi-tenant application where users may switch between tenant contexts, or where super-admin users can view tenant data. Cross-tenant data errors are both operationally dangerous and compliance-violating.
sources: Nexiby — Build a Multi-Tenant SaaS Application

title: Activity Log UI with Filterable Timeline
context: Anywhere an audit log is presented to end users — record history, compliance view, admin audit dashboard.
sources: Workato — Activity Audit Log, Aspirity — Admin Panel Design Best Practices

title: Guided Import with Template, Mapping, and Repair
context: Any import feature accepting external data files (CSV, Excel) for customer data, product catalogs, financial data, contact lists.
sources: Smart Interface Design Patterns — Bulk Import UX

title: Hierarchical Settings Architecture
context: Any enterprise application with settings that affect different audiences — system-wide, organization-level, and personal preferences.
sources: Aspirity — Admin Panel Design Best Practices

title: Surface Frequent Admin Actions Prominently
context: Admin panel design and ongoing redesign audits. If 80% of daily usage involves specific actions, those should be reachable within 2 clicks from the admin home.
sources: Allsite.pro — 10 Tips for Admin Panels

title: Consistent Date and Number Formatting
context: All date, number, and currency display throughout the application. Critical for global enterprise deployments and any application serving users in multiple countries.
sources: ROSSUL — Enterprise UX Best Practices

title: Role Hierarchy and Permission Inheritance
context: Organizations with clear job hierarchies where senior roles consistently have all the access of junior roles plus additional capabilities. Simplifies administration dramatically in complex multi-role systems.
sources: Glance Group — RBAC in Enterprise Apps

title: White-Label and Theme Customization for B2B SaaS
context: B2B SaaS applications offered to organizations that serve their own end users — embedded tools, reseller platforms, client portals.
sources: Workato — Seamless White-Label Integration, Nexiby — Multi-Tenant SaaS

title: Instant Preview for UI Configuration Changes
context: Theme editors, dashboard builders, report configurators, form builders — any setting with visual impact where users need to see the effect before committing.
sources: Aspirity — Admin Panel Design Best Practices

title: Print and Export Formatting
context: All reports, audit logs, approval records, financial statements, and executive dashboards in enterprise applications.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Dashboard UX

title: Impersonation and Support View Mode
context: Enterprise SaaS applications with support teams that handle customer issues. Also valuable for QA testing role-specific functionality.
sources: Nexiby — Multi-Tenant SaaS

title: Mobile-Aware Layouts for Field Workers
context: Enterprise applications with user personas who access the system away from their desk — field sales, operations, healthcare, logistics.
sources: UXpilot — Enterprise UX Best Practices

title: Rich Excel Export with Conditional Formatting
context: All Excel/CSV export functionality in data tables and reports where the exported file is used for reporting, presentation, or further analysis.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Data Table UX Patterns

title: Visibility of System Status
context: Apply any time the system performs an action with a consequence the user initiated — file saves, submissions, background processing, data loads. Essential whenever there is any latency between action and outcome.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, Laws of UX — Doherty Threshold

title: Error Messages — Plain Language with Constructive Solutions
context: Apply to all system errors, form validation messages, permission errors, empty result states, and API failures. Every error state is an opportunity to demonstrate respect for the user's time.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Error Message Guidelines, Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Design Guidelines for Errors in Forms, Component Gallery — Alert / Notification Banner, Eleken — Modal UX, About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 15 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Error Severity Categorization — Match Presentation to Impact
context: Use when designing the full notification and error system for any enterprise application. Prevents "error fatigue" where users reflexively dismiss all alerts.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Indicators, Validations, and Notifications, Component Gallery — Alert, Component Gallery — Toast, Stack Overflow UX Discussion

title: Provide Sufficient Context in Notifications
context: Apply to any notification triggered by asynchronous system events — batch job completion, assignment changes, alerts, approvals needed. Users may be focused elsewhere when notifications arrive.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Indicators, Validations, and Notifications

title: Opt-Out Confirmation for High-Consequence Actions
context: Apply to genuinely irreversible, high-consequence actions where undo is architecturally impossible — permanent data purge, account deletion, irreversible bulk writes.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, Good UI — Undo Instead of Confirmation Dialogs

title: Design Error Recovery Paths at Every Failure Point
context: Apply when specifying behavior for any interactive workflow — map all possible failure points and design explicit recovery paths for each.
sources: Boxes and Arrows — Foundations of Interaction Design, Laws of UX — Peak-End Rule

title: Inline Error Messages — Proximity to Source Field
context: Use for all form validation in all contexts.
sources: Stack Overflow UX Discussion, W3C WCAG 2.1

title: Graceful Degradation for Network Errors
context: Use in all web and mobile applications that make network requests, especially enterprise apps used in variable connectivity environments.
sources: Carbon Design System — Notification Pattern

title: Change Blindness — Mark State Changes Explicitly
context: Use when designing real-time data displays, status indicators, and alerting systems for monitoring or operational dashboards.
sources: 100 Things #8 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Habituation — Calibrate Alert Frequency to Maintain Urgency
context: Use when designing notification and alerting systems, especially for monitoring dashboards and operational applications with high event volume.
sources: 100 Things #43, #45 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Success State Confirmation
context: Use after all form submissions, save operations, exports, and any consequential user action.
sources: AlterSquare — Microinteractions

title: Undo Feedback Toast
context: After any destructive or high-impact action — delete, archive, move, status change — especially when undo is technically supported but not visually obvious.
sources: Eleken — Bulk Action UX

title: Error State Messaging with Resolution Guidance
context: All validation errors, system errors, permission errors, and failed operations throughout the application. Generic error messages are the most common source of unnecessary support tickets.
sources: Carbon Design System — Notifications

title: Unified Notification and Feedback System
context: Throughout the entire application. Consistent feedback patterns are a core indicator of design system maturity. Inconsistent feedback creates cognitive overhead as users parse what each notification type means.
sources: Carbon Design System — Notifications

title: Confirmation Dialogs for Destructive Actions
context: Any action that is difficult or impossible to reverse — permanently deleting records, bulk archiving, overwriting data, revoking permissions.
sources: Eleken — Bulk Action UX, Carbon Design System — Dialogs

title: Descriptive Wizard Navigation Button Labels
context: Use in all modal dialogs with primary and secondary actions, especially for destructive or consequential operations.
sources: Eleken — Modal UX, Nielsen Norman Group — Wizards: Definition and Design Recommendations

title: Notification Center with Categorization
context: Any enterprise application with background events, multi-user activity, approval workflows, or system alerts.
sources: Carbon Design System — Notifications

title: Error Prevention
context: Before designing error messages, first ask whether the error can be prevented entirely. Especially critical for irreversible or high-cost operations — data deletion, financial transactions, bulk writes.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 15 (Wiley, 2014), Nielsen Norman Group — Heuristic 5, UX Stack Exchange

title: Inline Validation — Validate on Field Blur, Not on Keystroke
context: Use when designing any data entry form, especially multi-field enterprise forms with complex validation rules.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 14 (Wiley, 2014), UX Stack Exchange, Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Design Guidelines for Errors in Forms, Good UI — Inline Validation

title: Postel's Law — Accept Varied Input, Output Consistent Data
context: Apply to all input fields with specific expected formats — phone numbers, dates, currencies, URLs, CSV imports, search fields — in any enterprise data entry context.
sources: Laws of UX — Postel's Law, Good UI — Forgiving Inputs

title: Keep Forms as Short as Possible — Eliminate Unnecessary Fields
context: Apply to all form design — registration, data entry, configuration, checkout, filtering dialogs. Each additional field adds cognitive burden and increases abandonment.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Web Form Design Top 10, Good UI — Fewer Form Fields, Laws of UX — Parkinson's Law

title: Single-Column Form Layouts and Logical Field Sequencing
context: Apply to all forms with more than 3 fields. Single-column layouts maintain a clear reading path and eliminate eye-tracking ambiguity.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Web Form Design Top 10

title: Group Related Form Fields With Section Headings
context: Apply to forms with more than ~6 fields, or forms covering multiple distinct topics (personal information, billing, shipping, preferences).
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Web Form Design Top 10, Laws of UX — Law of Common Region

title: Never Use Placeholder Text as a Substitute for Labels
context: Use when designing any form where the ratio of required to optional fields is significantly unbalanced in either direction.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group / Gov.uk research, Nielsen Norman Group — Web Form Design Top 10

title: Smart Defaults in Forms and Configuration
context: Apply to new record creation, report configuration, filter defaults, notification preferences, and user profile completion wherever context enables educated defaults.
sources: Good UI — Smart Defaults, Nielsen Norman Group — Minimize Cognitive Load

title: Appropriate Control Selection for Form Inputs
context: Use when designing form controls and data entry interfaces, and when reviewing existing forms for control type appropriateness.
sources: About Face Ch. 21 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Enterprise Form Design
context: All data entry forms in enterprise applications — record creation, settings configuration, wizard steps, import mapping, approval request forms.
sources: Carbon Design System — Forms

title: Match Input Field Type and Size to Expected Input
context: Apply to any form with varied input types — short IDs, addresses, descriptions, comments. Field size provides a visual affordance for expected input length.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Web Form Design Top 10

title: Progress Communication in Multi-Step Forms
context: Apply to multi-step or multi-page forms, forms requiring external documents, and forms taking more than 2–3 minutes. Uncertainty increases cognitive load and abandonment.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Web Form Design Top 10

title: Prioritized Multi-Error Display
context: Use when designing forms with multi-layer validation logic, especially in enterprise data entry tools where fields have format, logical, and cross-field consistency requirements.
sources: UX Stack Exchange / r/UXDesign

title: Save & Resume for Long Forms
context: Use for any form expected to take more than 3 minutes to complete or that requires information users may need to look up.
sources: FormAssembly — Multi-Step Forms

title: Parkinson's Law — Constrain Forms and Workflows to Reduce Task Inflation
context: Apply to form design, checkout/onboarding flow length, and complex wizard configuration where open-ended time availability leads to over-deliberation on simple decisions.
sources: Laws of UX — Parkinson's Law

title: Fitts's Law — Size and Proximity of Action Targets
context: Apply to all interactive element sizing and placement decisions — buttons, form fields, menu items, table row actions, toolbar icons. Critical in dense data-heavy enterprise UIs where users spend hours with mouse precision.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Fitts's Law and Its Applications in UX, Laws of UX — Fitts's Law

title: Progressive Disclosure — Show Core Features First, Defer Specialized Options
context: Use when designing feature-rich interfaces, when users complain about visual complexity, and when building dashboards, data tables, or configuration interfaces.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 10 (Wiley, 2014), Interaction Design Foundation, Nielsen Norman Group — Progressive Disclosure

title: Notifications — Indicators, Validations, and Notifications Taxonomy
context: Use when designing any application with multiple types of system messages and events.
sources: Carbon Design System — Notification Pattern, Nielsen Norman Group — Indicators, Validations, and Notifications

title: Design Every Interactive State — Not Just the Happy Path
context: Apply to specifying behavior for any interactive component or workflow in a complex enterprise application. Incomplete state design causes engineering to make arbitrary decisions that ship as UX failures.
sources: Boxes and Arrows — Foundations of Interaction Design

title: Direct Manipulation — Act Directly on UI Elements
context: Apply to data tables with editable cells, inline record name editing, drag-to-reorder lists, in-context status changes, and inline tag editing.
sources: Good UI — Direct Manipulation

title: Highlighting Recommended Options — Reduce Analysis Paralysis
context: Apply to pricing tiers, plan selection, template galleries, chart type pickers, permission level selectors, export format options — wherever users face multiple equivalent-looking choices.
sources: Good UI — Highlighting Recommended Options

title: Implementation Model vs. Mental Model — Bridge the Gap
context: Use when designing data-heavy applications, when users express confusion about "why the system works this way," and when designing navigation for complex domain models.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 2 (Wiley, 2014), 100 Things Every Designer Needs to Know About People (New Riders, 2011)

title: Eliminate Excise — Remove Non-Goal-Serving Work
context: Use during UX reviews of existing workflows, when users report tasks take "too many steps," and when designing any approval or data entry workflow.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 11 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Modal Editing Selection Framework (Inline / Modal / Full Page)
context: Use for every CRUD operation in a data-driven application to ensure the right editing surface is chosen for each operation type.
sources: Hacker News UX Discussion, Stack Overflow

title: Undo Enables Exploration
context: Use when designing any application with persistent data changes, and when evaluating the trade-off between confirmation dialogs and immediate action.
sources: About Face Ch. 16 (Wiley, 2014), LogRocket — UX for Reversible Actions

title: Soft Delete / Trash Pattern for Recoverable Deletion
context: Use for document deletion, record deletion, and any content removal where the recovery cost of accidental deletion is high.
sources: LogRocket — UX for Reversible Actions

title: Command Palette
context: Use in complex applications with many features where power users perform repeated actions, especially developer tools, productivity apps, and analytics platforms with 10+ distinct workflows.
sources: HashBuilds — Command Palette Pattern, Retool — Designing the Command Palette, Sam Solomon — Designing Command Palettes

title: Toast Notification Best Practices
context: Use for action confirmations, background process completions, and non-critical alerts that don't require immediate user action.
sources: SetProduct — Notifications UI Design

title: Human-in-the-Loop Copilot Pattern for AI
context: Use when designing any AI-powered feature in a product, regardless of the AI's actual capability level.
sources: Microsoft — Copilot UX Guidance

title: Affordances — Make Interaction Cues Visible
context: Use when designing interactive data tables, forms, and dashboards, and when users report being unaware of features or interactions.
sources: About Face Ch. 17 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Disabled State Design with Explanatory Tooltips
context: All interactive elements that may be conditionally disabled — buttons, form fields, menu items, navigation links, bulk actions.
sources: Carbon Design System — Disabled States

title: Dirty State Indicators and Unsaved Change Warnings
context: All forms, editors, configuration panels, and builders with editable state that is not auto-saved.
sources: Microsoft Docs — Desktop UX Guidelines

title: Multi-Select with Shift+Click and Ctrl+Click
context: All lists, tables, and grids in desktop enterprise applications that support multi-select. A non-negotiable pattern for power users who expect OS-native selection conventions.
sources: UX Patterns — Data Table

title: Inline Action Disclosure (Hover Reveal)
context: Table rows, list items, and cards where actions are contextual to specific items. Dense lists where showing all actions permanently would create visual noise.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Data Table UX Patterns

title: Peak-End Rule — Design Success States and Error Recovery With Care
context: Apply to onboarding completion, data export success states, form submission confirmations, end-of-wizard confirmations, and error recovery moments in enterprise apps.
sources: Laws of UX — Peak-End Rule

title: Contextual Menus — Efficiency Accelerators, Not Primary Pathways
context: Use in file managers, data tables, design tools, and complex applications with many per-item actions.
sources: Interaction Design Foundation — Microinteractions, Nielsen Norman Group — Contextual Menus: Delivering Relevant Tools for Tasks

title: Transitions and Animations — 150–300ms for State Changes
context: Apply to panel open/close, accordion expand/collapse, sidebar collapse, modal appearance/dismissal, tooltip show/hide, filter panel open, row expand.
sources: Good UI — Transitions and Animations, Microsoft Fluent 2 — Motion, AlterSquare — Microinteractions

title: Benefit-Oriented CTA Labels
context: Apply to upgrade prompts, feature adoption CTAs, subscription upgrade buttons, export/download CTAs, and new feature trial buttons.
sources: Good UI — Benefit-Oriented CTAs

title: Opt-Out Defaults for Beneficial Settings
context: Apply to email notification preferences, feature usage reporting, auto-save settings, default view configurations, and recommended integrations where the default state benefits the user.
sources: Good UI — Opt-Out vs. Opt-In Defaults

title: Exposing Options vs. Hiding in Dropdowns for Primary Choices
subcategory: Control selection for small option sets
context: Apply to filter controls with ≤5 options, tab/segmented control vs. dropdown decisions, and dashboard period selectors where user frequently switches between 2–5 options.
sources: Good UI — Exposing Options vs. Hiding in Dropdowns

title: Slide-Over Panels as Non-Blocking Modal Alternatives
context: Use when editing a record from a list where the user may need to compare with adjacent records, for settings panels, and for multi-field detail views in split-screen layouts.
sources: UX Stack Exchange / r/UXDesign

title: Version History for Collaborative Content
context: Use for document editors, configuration management, and any collaborative content system where content evolves across multiple users and sessions.
sources: LogRocket — UX for Reversible Actions

title: Optimistic UI Updates
context: Use for low-risk, high-frequency actions where server failures are rare — toggles, likes, quick status changes, marking items complete.
sources: Interaction Design Foundation — Microinteractions

title: Notification Center for High-Volume Events
context: Use in enterprise apps with active event systems — project management, CRMs, workflow automation platforms — that generate many background events.
sources: Courier — Notification Center Design

title: Microinteraction Four-Component Model
context: Use when designing any small interaction: button feedback, toggle switches, form validation states, loading indicators, and success states.
sources: Interaction Design Foundation — Microinteractions

title: Appropriate Friction for AI-Powered Actions
context: Use for AI operations that modify persistent data, send communications, or make consequential decisions.
sources: Microsoft — Copilot UX Guidance

title: AI Suggestion Autocomplete — Ghost Text Pattern
context: Use in search boxes, text editors, form fields with common patterns, and command palette inputs where AI can predict the user's likely completion.
sources: Touch4IT — Role of Microinteractions in UX

title: Transparency About AI Limitations
context: Use in all AI-powered features, especially where users may over-rely on AI-generated outputs or treat them as authoritative.
sources: Microsoft — Copilot UX Guidance

title: Visible Drag Affordances and Drop Zone Feedback
context: Any interface with reorderable items, Kanban boards, file uploads, visual builders, dashboard widget arrangement.
sources: Eleken — Drag and Drop UI, Smart Interface Design Patterns — Drag and Drop UX

title: Presence Indicators for Collaborative Editing
context: Use in any real-time collaborative editing surface where multiple users may be working simultaneously on the same document or record.
sources: UXPin — Real-Time Collaboration Tools

title: Von Restorff Effect — Visual Isolation for Primary Actions
context: Apply to primary CTA buttons, recommended pricing plans, critical alert messages, and required field markers. The most important action in any UI should stand apart visually.
sources: Laws of UX — Von Restorff Effect

title: Avoid Visual Clutter — Remove Redundant Elements
context: Apply during all design reviews, especially in data-dense enterprise dashboards and configuration screens. Each redundant element subtracts from attention available for meaningful content.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Minimize Cognitive Load to Maximize Usability, Good UI — Fewer Borders

title: Visual Hierarchy — Establish a Clear Type Scale
context: Apply to dashboard layouts, data tables, report output, settings pages, detail panels — any screen with multiple levels of information density.
sources: Good UI — Visual Hierarchy

title: Design for Scanning — Leverage F-Pattern and Layer-Cake Patterns
context: Use when designing page layouts, dashboard arrangements, and data table column ordering.
sources: 100 Things #6 (New Riders, 2011), Nielsen Norman Group — F-Shaped Pattern for Reading Web Content

title: Make Important Information Visually Salient
subcategory: Visual salience and critical information emphasis
context: Apply to dashboards, alert systems, status indicators, and dense data views where critical information must be found quickly during visual search.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications

title: Visual Hierarchy Through Grouping and Weight
context: Use when designing dashboards, data tables, and any complex screen, and when users report difficulty identifying what to do or look at on a screen.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 13 (Wiley, 2014), 100 Things #1 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Avoid Visual Noise — Reduce Without Losing Capability
context: Use during visual design review of data-heavy screens, when users describe interfaces as "overwhelming" or "busy," and when preparing existing interfaces for density improvements.
sources: About Face 4th Ed., Ch. 13 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Proximity Creates Grouping — Gestalt Principle
context: Use when designing form layouts, dashboard cards, data tables, and any multi-section screen where visual grouping must replace decorative borders.
sources: 100 Things #9 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design
context: Apply during design review passes, especially in data-dense enterprise dashboards and configuration screens where feature creep creates visual competition for attention.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, Laws of UX — Occam's Razor, Laws of UX — Law of Prägnanz

title: Serial Position Effect — Place Critical Items First and Last
context: Apply to navigation menu ordering, toolbar button placement, table column ordering, pricing plan ordering, and step sequencing in wizards.
sources: Laws of UX — Serial Position Effect

title: Sticky Primary CTAs on Long Pages
context: Apply to long configuration pages, multi-section forms, and detailed record views with a primary action (Save, Submit, Approve) that must remain accessible at all times.
sources: Good UI — Sticky / Persistent CTA, Good UI — CTA Repetition

title: Avoid False Bottoms — Visual Continuity in Scrollable Views
context: Apply to long dashboard pages, infinite scroll tables, report output pages, analytics views, and documentation pages. False bottoms kill content discovery.
sources: Good UI — Visual Continuity

title: Peripheral Vision Determines Dashboard Character
context: Use when designing dashboard layouts and evaluating whether animations are appropriate for enterprise contexts.
sources: 100 Things #2 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Skeleton Screens for Full-Page Loads (2–10 Seconds)
context: Use for page-level loads, card grids, data tables, dashboard widgets, and any container-based layout where content structure is known before data arrives.
sources: LogRocket — Skeleton Loading Screens, Nielsen Norman Group — Skeleton Screens 101, Component Gallery — Skeleton Loader, Laws of UX — Doherty Threshold, Smashing Magazine — UX Strategies for Real-Time Dashboards

title: Match Loading Indicator to Operation Type and Duration
context: Apply any time a user must wait for a system operation. Mismatching indicator type to operation context causes confusion about what the system is doing.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Skeleton Screens 101, Good UI — Faster Load Times / Progress Indicators, Component Gallery — Progress Bar, Carbon Design System — Loading

title: Doherty Threshold — Target Sub-400ms Response for Interactive Operations
context: Apply to all interactive operations in data-heavy applications — search queries, filter application, record saves, chart renders, table sorts, API calls, form validation.
sources: Laws of UX — Doherty Threshold

title: Perceived Progress Reduces Wait Frustration
context: Use when designing loading states for data-heavy queries, long-running processes, and any operation where users must wait more than 2 seconds.
sources: 100 Things #77 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Performance Budgeting for Data-Heavy Interfaces
context: During design (IA choices affect performance), development (rendering strategy), and QA (performance regression testing). Performance is a UX feature — slow interfaces are productivity bottlenecks.
sources: UX Patterns — Data Table, ROSSUL — Enterprise UX

title: Progress Bars for Deterministic Operations
context: Use for file uploads, batch data imports/exports, any multi-phase operation where server-side progress can be communicated in real time.
sources: LogRocket — Skeleton Loading Screens

title: Spinners for Short, Simple Loading States
context: Use for button loading states after submission, small component updates, and non-structural asynchronous operations under 3 seconds.
sources: LogRocket — Skeleton Loading Screens

title: Time Perception — Accurate Estimates Reduce Frustration
context: Use when designing progress feedback for long-running operations, and when setting user expectations for report generation, data export, or batch processing.
sources: 100 Things #36, #94 (New Riders, 2011)

title: Left-Side Vertical Navigation for Complex Information Architectures
context: Use for enterprise applications with complex, multi-section navigation: ERP systems, analytics platforms, admin consoles, multi-module SaaS tools with 7+ top-level sections.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Left-Side Vertical Navigation on Desktop, Component Gallery — Navigation, Smashing Magazine — Breadcrumbs UX, Merveilleux.design — 6 Types of UX Navigation for SaaS, Aspirity — Admin Panel Design

title: Always Expose Navigation on Desktop — No Hamburger Menus
context: Apply to all desktop applications. Hiding navigation in hamburger menus on desktop reduces usage, increases time-to-navigation, degrades content discoverability, and increases task difficulty.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Navigation Discoverability on Desktop

title: Front-Load Navigation Labels With Information-Rich Keywords
context: Apply to all navigation labels — menus, breadcrumbs, sidebar items, tab labels, dropdown menus. Users scan navigation, they don't read it.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Left-Side Vertical Navigation on Desktop

title: Recently Viewed Records and Resume Incomplete Workflows
context: Apply to home/dashboard screens, navigation panels, and module entry screens to support the re-finding information-seeking mode.
sources: Good UI — Cart Reminder / Recently Viewed, Laws of UX — Zeigarnik Effect

title: Breadcrumbs for Deep Navigation Hierarchies
context: Use for applications with 3+ levels of navigation hierarchy, especially when users frequently arrive at deep records via search or deep links rather than top-down navigation.
sources: Smashing Magazine — Breadcrumbs UX Design, About Face Ch. 11 (Wiley, 2014), Component Gallery — Breadcrumbs, Merveilleux.design — 6 Types of UX Navigation for SaaS

title: Help Users Maintain Orientation in Complex Applications
context: Apply to any enterprise application with 3+ levels of navigation depth. Users lose orientation in complex apps and the resulting confusion leads to abandonment.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, Component Gallery — Breadcrumbs

title: Three-Level Navigation: Global / Contextual / Local
context: Use when structuring navigation for complex enterprise applications with deeply nested content or multiple parallel workflows.
sources: Smashing Magazine — Breadcrumbs UX Design

title: Active State Visibility in Navigation
context: Use in all navigation components in all application contexts without exception.
sources: Smashing Magazine — Breadcrumbs UX

title: Reducing Distance Between Intent and Action
context: Use when analyzing navigation efficiency, and when designing features performed multiple times daily.
sources: Smashing Magazine — Breadcrumbs UX

title: Contextual Secondary Navigation via Tabs
context: When a screen has 2–7 distinct content categories users switch between frequently. Do not use for content that should be viewed in sequence (use a wizard) or for more than 7 items (use a secondary sidebar instead).
sources: Merveilleux.design — 6 Types of UX Navigation for SaaS

title: Progressive Disclosure for Complex Feature Sets
context: Complex forms, configuration panels, settings pages, filter interfaces, and any feature with both simple and advanced usage modes.
sources: Interaction Design Foundation — Progressive Disclosure, UXPin — What is Progressive Disclosure?

title: In-Page Navigation for Long Single-Page Views
context: Apply to long settings pages, comprehensive record detail views, multi-section dashboard pages, documentation articles with 4+ distinct sections.
sources: Good UI — Page-Level Navigation

title: Search as Primary Navigation
context: Large complex enterprise applications with many navigational destinations or experienced power users who know what they want and prefer speed over discovery.
sources: Merveilleux.design — 6 Types of UX Navigation for SaaS

title: In-App Help Panel (Help Hub)
