context: All enterprise applications with more than basic functionality. Especially important when serving non-technical users in professional contexts.
sources: UXpilot — Enterprise UX Best Practices

title: Contextual "Help for This Page" Auto-Surfacing
context: All enterprise applications. Contextual help significantly reduces support ticket volume for common workflows by surfacing the right answer at the right moment.
sources: Document360 — Contextual In-App Documentation, Docsie — Context-Sensitive Help

title: Deep Linking and URL State Persistence
context: All data tables, dashboards, record detail views, and any view with configurable state. Essential for collaborative workflows where users share links to specific filtered views.
sources: UX Patterns — Data Table

title: Empty States — Communicate System Status Honestly
subcategory: Empty state design — learnability and activation
context: Apply to any feature panel that begins empty for new users: alerts, dashboards, saved reports, favorites, recently viewed — the highest-leverage moment for in-context education.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Designing Empty States in Complex Applications, Component Gallery — Empty State, Good UI — Zero-Data / Empty States, Interaction Design Foundation — Microinteractions, 100 Things #34 (New Riders, 2011), Eleken — Empty State UX, Carbon Design System — Empty States

title: Promote Learning by Doing — Safe Exploration
context: Apply during the initial use phase and ongoing discovery of advanced features in any complex desktop application.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications

title: Contextual Tooltip Help System
context: All complex forms, settings pages, data entry fields with specific format requirements, and features with non-obvious behavior. Essential in admin panels and configuration interfaces.
sources: UXpilot — Enterprise UX Best Practices, Document360 — Contextual In-App Documentation

title: Help and Documentation — Contextual and Task-Focused
context: Apply in complex enterprise tools with non-obvious workflows, advanced features, and specialized domain concepts where users cannot intuit the system.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics

title: Gradual Engagement — Demonstrate Value Before Commitment
context: Apply to free trial onboarding, new module adoption within an existing product, and exploring advanced features where demonstrating value before asking for commitment increases conversion.
sources: Good UI — Gradual Engagement

title: Promote Learnability Through In-Context Tips and Cues
context: Apply throughout the product lifecycle, not just during onboarding. Expert users plateau — in-context tips resurface capability they missed or forgot.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications, Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics

title: Learnability Through Concrete Examples
context: Use when designing onboarding experiences, help content, and empty states for any complex feature.
sources: 100 Things #34 (New Riders, 2011)

title: First-Run Experience and Pre-Populated Samples
context: Complex enterprise applications where the value proposition is not immediately obvious with an empty interface — project management tools, CRMs, BI platforms.
sources: Eleken — Empty State UX

title: Role-Based Onboarding Flows
context: All enterprise applications with distinct user roles that have different primary use cases — critical when the same platform serves technical admins and non-technical end users.
sources: Code Theorem — SaaS Onboarding UX, Formbricks — User Onboarding Best Practices

title: Progressive Onboarding (Feature Discovery Over Time)
context: Complex enterprise applications with many features where a complete upfront tour would be overwhelming or take more than 5 minutes to cover all features.
sources: Formbricks — User Onboarding Best Practices

title: Skippable, Optional Product Tours
context: First-login experience for genuinely new users. Also offer a "Take the tour" option for returning users exploring new features after an update.
sources: Formbricks — User Onboarding Best Practices

title: Celebratory Empty States (Zero-State Completion)
context: Task queues, approval lists, notification inboxes, and support ticket queues — any list representing work items to be processed where emptiness means completion.
sources: Eleken — Empty State UX, Thom McCarthy — Mastering Empty States UX

title: Tesler's Law — Absorb Complexity Into the System
context: Apply when simplifying a feature — data import/export flows, report configuration, permission systems, workflow builders, formula editors. Complexity can be shifted but not eliminated.
sources: Laws of UX — Tesler's Law

title: Pareto Principle — Prioritize the 20% Used 80% of the Time
context: Apply to feature prioritization, UX audit triage, navigation design, performance optimization, and test coverage planning for any enterprise application.
sources: Laws of UX — Pareto Principle

title: Appetite Setting — Time Budget Before Solution Design
context: Use at the beginning of any new feature or initiative, before any wireframing or technical scoping begins.
sources: Shape Up by Ryan Singer, Ch. 2 (Basecamp, 2019)

title: Identifying and Eliminating Rabbit Holes Before Building
context: Use after sketching a solution but before committing to building it — and during design reviews for any feature containing novel technical or design elements.
sources: Shape Up by Ryan Singer, Ch. 5 (Basecamp, 2019)

title: Vertical Slicing — Get One Piece Done First
context: Use at the start of every building cycle to validate the overall approach and build team momentum early.
sources: Shape Up by Ryan Singer, Ch. 11 (Basecamp, 2019)

title: Jobs-to-Be-Done Framework
context: Use during product discovery, feature prioritization, and any time the team is debating what to build next or when user feature requests seem disconnected from real needs.
sources: ProductPlan — Product Management Frameworks, UX Primer — JTBD in UX

title: Continuous Research Cadence
context: Use when establishing product team norms, and when research is currently ad hoc or only done for major launches.
sources: User Interviews — UX Research for PMs

title: RICE Scoring for Feature Prioritization
context: Use when prioritizing competing feature investments and when stakeholder pressure is distorting prioritization toward high-visibility but low-impact work.
sources: ProductPlan — Product Management Frameworks

title: MoSCoW Prioritization for Release Scoping
context: Use when planning release scope and when stakeholders are escalating features to "must-have" status without justification.
sources: ProductPlan — Product Management Frameworks

title: Merging Similar Functions — Reduce Feature Fragmentation
context: Apply to mature enterprise products with feature sprawl — consolidate redundant filters, duplicate export options, overlapping report types, and redundant navigation taxonomy.
sources: Good UI — Merging Similar Functions

title: Kano Model for Feature Prioritization
context: Use for feature roadmap prioritization, product discovery, and making trade-offs between investment areas.
sources: ProductPlan — Kano Model

title: Story Mapping for End-to-End Workflow Design
context: Use when planning a new product or major feature set and when defining MVP scope for complex workflows.
sources: ProductPlan — Product Management Frameworks

title: OKRs for Design Quality Goals
context: Use during quarterly planning cycles and when making the case for UX investment or establishing a design quality culture.
sources: ProductPlan — Product Management Frameworks

title: HEART Framework for UX Quality Measurement
context: Use when defining success metrics for a product or feature, and when building the case for UX investment in business terms.
sources: ProductPlan — Product Management Frameworks

title: Dual-Track Agile — Continuous Discovery and Delivery
context: Use when establishing product development process and when discovery and delivery are currently sequential, creating bottlenecks.
sources: ProductPlan — Product Management Frameworks

title: Loss Aversion Framing for Upgrade and Retention Prompts
context: Apply to feature trial expiry prompts, plan downgrade warnings, data retention notices, and subscription renewal reminders.
sources: Good UI — Loss Aversion Framing

title: Hill Charts for Progress Tracking
context: Use throughout a building cycle for progress tracking, especially when communicating project status to leadership.
sources: Shape Up by Ryan Singer, Ch. 13 (Basecamp, 2019)

title: Intrinsic Motivation Over Extrinsic Rewards in Enterprise Tools
context: Use when evaluating whether to add gamification elements to enterprise tools and when designing for long-term user engagement.
sources: 100 Things #54–55 (New Riders, 2011)

title: IDEO Iceberg Model for Systems Thinking in Design
context: Use when solving recurring problems that surface-level fixes haven't resolved, and when scoping a new product initiative with systemic complexity.
sources: IDEO Strategy Frameworks

title: Provide Navigation Alongside Search — Not Search Alone
context: Apply to any application with a large content or data set where users may approach with varying levels of familiarity — search alone fails exploratory and exhaustive seeking modes.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Search Is Not Enough

title: Filter Categories — Appropriate, Predictable, and Prioritized
context: Apply to any filtering or faceted navigation system in enterprise applications. Filters are only as valuable as their alignment with user mental models.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Helpful Filter Categories and Values, Good UI — Visible Filters

title: Faceted Search — Show Filters and Results Simultaneously
context: Apply to all faceted search implementations in enterprise data management, content management, and any large record set where progressive filtering is the primary navigation mode.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Filters vs. Facets, A List Apart — Design Patterns: Faceted Navigation

title: Autocomplete and Autosuggestion in Search
context: Apply to global search, record lookup fields, tag/label inputs, user assignment fields, and filter value inputs in enterprise applications.
sources: Good UI — Auto-Suggest / Autocomplete, Component Gallery — Combobox / Autocomplete, Nielsen Norman Group — Search Is Not Enough

title: Persistent Global Search Across All Entities
context: Use in any enterprise application with 3+ entity types and significant data volume, especially where users cannot always predict where data is stored.
sources: HashBuilds — Command Palette Pattern, About Face Ch. 14 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Faceted Filtering Over Browse Hierarchies
context: Use when designing search and retrieval for data-rich applications and when users report difficulty finding records in hierarchical navigation.
sources: About Face Ch. 14 (Wiley, 2014)

title: Per-Column Filtering and Global Search
context: Any table with more than 50 rows or more than one dimension users may want to filter on. Without filtering, users must manually scroll through hundreds of records to find relevant items.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables, UX Patterns — Data Table

title: Saved Filters and Search Views
context: Any enterprise application where users repeat the same filter combinations. Baseline feature for CRM, project management, analytics, and operations tools.
sources: Pencil & Paper — Data Table UX Patterns

title: Global Search with Federated Results
context: Any enterprise application with multiple object types and more than 1,000 total records across the system.
sources: Merveilleux.design — SaaS Navigation Types

title: Filter Panel Persistence and Active Filter Badges
context: All filterable data views in enterprise applications. Filter persistence prevents the frustrating "lost context" experience when users return to a view.
sources: UX Patterns — Data Table, Nielsen Norman Group — Data Tables

title: Design for All Four Information-Seeking Modes
subcategory: Information architecture for varied seeking behaviors
context: Apply to IA and navigation design for complex systems with large content or data sets where users approach with varying levels of familiarity and intent.
sources: Boxes and Arrows — Four Modes of Seeking Information

title: Match Between System and the Real World
context: Apply when writing UI labels, status messages, menu items, error text, and any user-facing copy — especially in enterprise tools where internal system terminology diverges from user vocabulary.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics

title: User Control and Freedom — Undo Over Confirmation
context: Apply for any destructive or hard-to-reverse action: deleting records, navigating away from unsaved work, bulk operations, archiving, sending communications.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, Good UI — Undo Instead of Confirmation Dialogs, Reddit — How I implemented an Undo/Redo system in a large complex visual application

title: Map Between System and Real World — Natural Mapping for Controls
context: Apply specifically to control mapping — sliders, toggles, directional buttons — where the physical relationship between control and outcome must match user expectations.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — 10 Usability Heuristics, Laws of UX — Jakob's Law

title: Zeigarnik Effect — Persist Incomplete Tasks and Draft States
context: Apply for draft/autosave indicators, incomplete workflow notifications, setup checklist reminders, abandoned report configurations, and partially completed import flows.
sources: Laws of UX — Zeigarnik Effect, Eleken — Wizard UI Pattern

title: Staged Disclosure for Linear Multi-Step Processes
context: Apply to one-time or infrequent linear processes: product setup, account configuration, checkout, application submissions, compliance workflows.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Progressive Disclosure

title: Wizards — Use for Novice Users and Infrequent Complex Processes
context: Use for forms with 15+ fields, infrequent tasks, branching logic, or untrained users. Not recommended for expert users who perform the task frequently.
sources: Andrew Coyle — Form Wizard Design, FormAssembly, Nielsen Norman Group — Wizards: Definition and Design Recommendations, Eleken — Wizard UI Pattern

title: Wizard Step Indicators — Communicate the Full Process
context: Use in any multi-step form, wizard, or onboarding flow with more than 2 steps. Also applies to approval workflows and any sequential process with defined stages.
sources: FormAssembly — Multi-Step Forms, 100 Things — Goal Gradient Effect (New Riders, 2011), Nielsen Norman Group — Wizards: Definition and Design Recommendations, Component Gallery — Stepper / Progress Tracker, Laws of UX — Goal-Gradient Effect, Andrew Coyle — How to Design a Form Wizard

title: Approval Status Visualization
context: Any enterprise workflow involving sequential approvals — purchase orders, expense reports, leave requests, content publishing, compliance reviews.
sources: Shyft — Mastering Approval Workflows: Approval Status Tracking

title: Prioritized Work Queue with Status Visibility
context: Support ticketing systems, operations dashboards, manufacturing work queues, financial review queues — any context where workers process items in order of priority.
sources: UXpilot — Enterprise UX Design Best Practices

title: Self-Sufficient Wizard Steps — Include Relevant Context Per Step
context: Apply to all wizard steps, especially those requiring comparison to prior selections. Users cannot easily reference information from prior steps when only one step is visible.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group — Wizards: Definition and Design Recommendations, Boxes and Arrows — The Shallow Dive

title: Flexible and Fluid Pathways Through Complex Workflows
context: Use for wizards with 5+ steps serving experienced users who may need to complete steps out of order, or configuration flows where some steps are optional.
sources: Eleken — Wizard UI Pattern, Nielsen Norman Group — 8 Guidelines for Complex Applications

title: Define Information Carried Between Workflow Steps
context: Apply to any multi-step workflow design in enterprise applications — configuration wizards, data transformation flows, approval chains. Information persistence is a critical design decision, not an implementation detail.
sources: Boxes and Arrows — The Shallow Dive

title: Conditional Branching in Workflows
context: Use when a workflow serves multiple user types or scenarios (e.g., individual vs. business account, new vs. returning customer), or when certain steps only apply under specific conditions.
sources: Andrew Coyle — How to Design a Form Wizard

title: Eligibility Transparency in Bulk Actions
context: Workflows where eligibility rules exist — only "draft" items can be published, only unlocked records can be deleted, only items owned by the current user can be exported.
sources: Eleken — Bulk Action UX

title: Cross-Page Bulk Selection
context: Any paginated data set where bulk operations are supported. Especially important in tables with more than 100 records or server-side pagination where "Select All" may mean vastly different things.
sources: Eleken — Bulk Action UX, Smart Interface Design Patterns — Bulk UX

title: Bulk Import Five-Stage Workflow
context: Any enterprise application that accepts data uploads (CSV, Excel, JSON) — CRMs, inventory systems, HR tools, financial platforms. Data imports are high-risk operations that require structured guidance.
sources: Smart Interface Design Patterns — How To Design Bulk Import UX

title: Wizard vs. Inline Bulk Workflow Selection
context: Use when deciding how to implement a bulk operation — calibrate to the complexity and risk of the action, not a single default approach.
sources: Eleken — Bulk Action UX

title: Conditional Approval Routing
context: Any approval workflow with varying levels of risk or value — expenses, POs, leave requests, IT changes. Most relevant in finance, HR, and IT change management.
sources: Shyft — Mastering Approval Workflows

title: Delegation and Backup Approvers
context: Any approval workflow where bottlenecks occur during approver absence — holidays, illness, travel. Without delegation, a single absent approver can halt entire business processes.
sources: Shyft — Mastering Approval Workflows

title: Asynchronous Bulk Processing with Job Monitoring
context: Any bulk operation that may take more than 2 seconds, or any background task — report generation, data exports, email campaigns, schema migrations.
sources: GeeksforGeeks — Task Queues System Design

title: Multi-Level Undo in Visual/Configuration Builders
context: Visual editors, drag-and-drop builders, and configuration interfaces where users make iterative changes over an extended session.
sources: YouTube — Handling Undo/Redo in Enterprise Applications

title: Context-Aware Undo Scope
context: Complex enterprise tools where multiple editing contexts coexist simultaneously, such as a main canvas with a floating property panel or a parent grid with a nested modal editor.
sources: Reddit — How I implemented an Undo/Redo system in a large complex visual application

title: Linear vs. Selective Undo Models
context: Default to linear undo for most applications. Consider selective undo only in specialized collaborative or multi-workspace environments where strict linear ordering breaks user expectations.
sources: Prague University — Management of Undo/Redo Operations in Complex Environments

title: Reciprocity Through Value-First Product Design
context: Apply when designing freemium funnels, free trial flows, or content marketing strategies where conversion from free to paid is a core growth metric. Most effective for SaaS and subscription products where users need to experience real value before committing.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group – Contextual Upselling | CXL – Cialdini's 7 Principles of Persuasion | Irrational Labs – Behavioral Design | Monetizely – Reciprocity in SaaS

title: Progressive Commitment via Multi-Step Onboarding
context: Essential for onboarding flows, lead generation forms, subscription signups, and any multi-step conversion process. Particularly effective when the desired end-state action (purchase, account creation) would feel high-stakes if presented upfront.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group – Commitment and Consistency in UX | IvyForms – Multi-Step vs Single-Step Forms | Auth0 – Progressive Profiling | CXL – Cialdini Commitment Principle

title: Social Proof Patterns: Reviews, Counters, and Activity Feeds
context: Apply at every major decision point: pricing pages, checkout, feature adoption prompts, and upgrade nudges. Most critical for new products or features where users have no personal frame of reference and uncertainty is highest.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group – Social Proof in UX | UX Collective – Social Proof as Ancestral Habit | LanderMagic – Social Proofing Examples | Growth.Design – Psychology Principles

title: Authority Signals: Badges, Certifications, and Expert Endorsements
context: Critical for products in regulated industries (finance, healthcare, legal) and for any product where users are handing over sensitive data or making significant financial commitments. Authority signals also help overcome trust deficits for newer or less well-known products.
sources: CXL – Authority Principle | Brand VM – Trust Signals in Web Design | Jasmine Directory – UX Design for Credibility | LogRocket – Persuasive Design Responsibly

title: Scarcity and Urgency Signals for Faster Decision-Making
context: Most effective at or near the conversion point — pricing pages, checkout, and upgrade prompts. Works best when the user has already expressed intent (added to cart, started a free trial) and needs a final nudge to commit.
sources: UI-Patterns – Scarcity Design Pattern | CXL – Scarcity Principle | LogRocket – Persuasive Design

title: Loss Aversion Framing for Upgrades and Warnings
context: Apply when users need a motivational push to upgrade, enable a security feature, or complete a critical workflow. Particularly effective for free-to-paid conversion nudges, security prompts, and deadline-driven workflows.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group – Prospect Theory and Loss Aversion | Interaction Design Foundation – Loss Aversion | Muzli – How Framing Affects Decisions | Coglode – Loss Aversion Nugget

title: Price Anchoring and Decoy Pricing Architecture
context: Essential for pricing page design, subscription tier architecture, and any scenario where multiple options with price differences are presented. Also applies to feature comparison tables where presenting high-value features first anchors perceived product value.
sources: Interaction Design Foundation – Anchoring | PayPro Global – Price Anchoring for SaaS | SBI Growth – Price Anchoring Strategy | Krotov Studio – Pricing Pages That Convert

title: The Endowment Effect: Ownership Psychology in Onboarding
context: Most powerful in the early product journey — first session and first week. Effective for SaaS tools where users create and manage ongoing work (design tools, project management, CRMs). Also critical for reducing free-trial-to-paid churn.
sources: Nielsen Norman Group – Endowment Effect in UX | Learning Loop – Endowment Effect | LinkedIn – Endowment Effect in UX | UX Collective – Design by Default

title: Status Quo Bias and Strategic Default Settings
context: Apply to all setting configurations, preference screens, email subscription management, and pricing plan selection. Also critical during product migrations, feature deprecations, and plan restructuring where retaining existing users is the goal.
sources: UI-Patterns – Status Quo Bias | UX Collective – Design by Default | Prosci – Nudge Theory | Coglode – Status Quo Bias Nugget

title: Nudge Theory and Choice Architecture Design
context: Applies to every decision point in a product: onboarding flows, settings pages, upgrade prompts, checkout, and notification preferences. Especially valuable when users face information overload or decision fatigue and benefit from a guided path.
sources: Blink UX – Design Nudges for Behavior Change | Kite Metric – Nudge Theory in UX | UX Collective – Nudging as Design Method | Prosci – Nudge Theory and Choice Architecture

title: System 1 Design: Reducing Cognitive Load for Intuitive UIs
context: Foundational to all UI design. Especially critical for enterprise and productivity tools used by cognitively busy workers, and for any high-frequency interaction where users want minimal friction. Reversals (delete confirmations, permission warnings) are the correct place to engage System 2.
sources: Motivus – Kahneman Two-System Concept in UX | UX Collective – Better Decisions: Two Systems | Webflow – UX Lessons from Thinking Fast and Slow | mm-software – How Decision Processes Guide Interface Design

title: BJ Fogg's Behavior Model: Motivation, Ability, and Prompts
context: Use as a diagnostic framework when analyzing feature adoption failures or drop-off points in user flows. Particularly valuable for onboarding design, re-engagement campaigns, and any feature where the desired behavior requires a behavior change from the user.
sources: Northbeam – Fogg Behavior Model | UI-Patterns – Making the Fogg Model Actionable | The Decision Lab – Fogg Behavior Model | Rock Health – Behavior Design with BJ Fogg

title: Goal Gradient Effect: Progress Visibility to Accelerate Completion
context: Highly effective for onboarding completion flows, profile setup, course or training completion, loyalty programs, and any multi-session journey where users may disengage between sessions. The effect is most pronounced in the final 20–30% of the journey.
sources: LogRocket – Goal Gradient Effect | UX Bulletin – Goal-Gradient Effect | Raw Studio – Goal Gradient Effect | Laws of UX

title: Zeigarnik Effect: Unfinished Tasks Drive Return Engagement
context: Apply to onboarding flows, course and training products, e-commerce checkout, and any multi-session workflow. Particularly powerful for re-engagement campaigns where users have shown intent but not completed a desired action.
sources: NNgroup – Zeigarnik Effect in UX (YouTube) | UX Collective – Zeigarnik Effect in Design | GeeksforGeeks – Zeigarnik Effect in UX | Coglode – Zeigarnik Effect Nugget

title: Peak-End Rule: Engineering Memorable Session Moments
context: Most applicable to linear, sequential experiences: onboarding flows, checkout processes, customer service interactions, and session endings. Also valuable for product launches and major feature releases where the first-time experience sets a lasting memory.
sources: Laws of UX – Peak-End Rule | Webflow – UX Lessons from Thinking Fast and Slow | Umbrex – Peak-End Rule in Experience Design | Design Psychologist Podcast – Peak-End Rule

title: Hick's Law and Decision Fatigue Reduction
context: Critical for onboarding flows, settings screens, and any UI with a large feature set or product catalogue. Also applies to checkout flows, navigation architecture, and dashboard design for enterprise tools.
sources: Heurilens – Decision Fatigue in Interfaces | Vrunik Design – Designing for Decision Fatigue | Laws of UX – Hick's Law | mm-software – Decision Processes and Interface Design

title: Paradox of Choice: Curated Paths and Reduced Option Sets
context: Essential for any product with a large catalogue (e-commerce, content libraries, feature-rich SaaS), pricing page design, and onboarding personalization flows. Also applies to navigation architecture and feature discovery in complex applications.
sources: UserTesting – Paradox of Choice in UX | LinkedIn – Paradox of Choice in Digital Product Design | Laws of UX – Paradox of Choice | Robert Ladkani Design – Psychology of Choice

title: Progressive Profiling: Gradually Building User Data Over Time
context: Ideal for SaaS platforms, B2B tools, and any product where user segmentation improves the product experience. Most important during the acquisition and early activation phases. Also effective for re-engaging existing users who provided minimal information at sign-up.
sources: Auth0 – Progressive Profiling | Descope – Progressive Profiling 101 | Monday.com – Lead Generation Forms | Nielsen Norman Group – Commitment and Consistency
